data.title: Murphy USA: Attractively Valued Thanks To Smart Capital Allocation
data.description:
The Murphy USA Investment Thesis

Murphy USA Inc. (NYSE:MUSA) is an overlooked investment because many stock screeners disregard slow-growing companies. But as the graph above shows, these companies can produce excellent results, leaving the S&P 500 (SPY) far behind.

The secret to the success of these under-the-radar companies is that they are often very shareholder-friendly and return a lot of capital to shareholders. In the case of Murphy, this is done through buybacks. And as long as Murphy continues to implement this strategy and buy back shares, I see no reason why it shouldn't be attractive to long-term investors. Plus, if the opportunity for multiple expansion arises, its potential increases even further.

But at the current price, which I consider fair, I don't see any potential for multiple expansion. So my thesis remains that share buybacks will promote EPS growth and are the most important point in the investment thesis.

What was my last thesis on MUSA about?

In October of last year, I wrote an article about Murphy, in which I expected that Murphy would continue to be a share cannibal. In this context, share cannibal refers to a company that aggressively repurchases its own shares and returns money to shareholders.

And that's exactly what Murphy did. They continued to hold on to their capital allocation strategy and repurchase shares.

However, I had one criticism at the time: I didn't see much room for multiple expansion, which made me feel like the upside was somewhat limited.

Since Murphy's share price has decreased since October, now is a good time to investigate why this happened and whether there's an opportunity for multiple expansion.

For this reason, I would now like to take another look at Murphy and see what has changed since then.

Murphy USA's Q1/2025 Results

The stock was sold off a bit after Murphy's Double Miss, which missed EPS targets by $1.33 and revenue targets by $160 million. The main reason for this was lower fuel prices, which resulted in lower revenue from petroleum products than in the previous year. Additionally, there were calendar effects that caused a volume decline.

Although expenses have also decreased compared to last year, unfortunately, these decreases could not offset the decrease in sales, resulting in a lower net income compared to last year.

However, it should be noted that store and other operating costs have only increased slightly, and costs for goods sold have even decreased, despite the fact that we are still in an environment where inflation is an issue.

Depending on how the situation in Israel/Iran develops and its potential impact on the oil price, it would not surprise me if we experience significant fluctuations in petroleum product sales in 2025. If the oil price were to rise slightly, the product sales would probably increase again. However, if the oil price were to rise too much, it would negatively impact sales. So this could therefore lead to temporary fluctuations.

MUSA's Balance Sheet

The cash position increased slightly from $47 million to $49 million in the last quarter. Similarly, inventories were reduced by nearly $37 million, but unfortunately, long-term debt increased by ~$142 million.

The main reason for this is the increased use of the revolving credit facility, which matures in 2026 and has a weighted interest rate of 6.59%. In this quarter, $200 million was borrowed, while at the end of last year, it was only $56 million. Therefore, I see the balance sheet as weaker than at the end of last year.

But overall, the balance sheet is still relatively secure, as I think that the outstanding liabilities are adequately covered by the net income.

MUSA's Capital Allocation

Murphy spent $151 million on stock buybacks, purchasing approximately 312k shares last quarter. This shows that Murphy's strategy continues to clearly focus on stock buybacks.

As the above diagram shows, these share buybacks actually reduce the outstanding share count and do not only serve to offset SBC. Therefore, I believe these stock buybacks positively affect shareholders.

The opening of new stores and further investments also seem to have a positive effect, as the ROIC stands at a strong 18%. However, it should be noted that ROICs were much higher in the past. So it is important to monitor how this develops in the future. If the ROIC continues to fall, it could be a warning sign.

MUSA Stock Valuation

Although Murphy's valuation remains above the five-year average of 13.9x, it has fallen from its five-year high of 22x to now 17x. The big question now is whether the normalized earnings are higher, which would make the multiple lower, or if the current earnings are representative.

If earnings were higher on a normalized basis, we could assume the potential for multiple expansion or rapidly increasing sales. However, since the situation is difficult to predict, I don't believe multiple expansion is likely.

For this reason, I currently consider the shares to be fairly valued, especially given that Alimentation Couche-Tard (OTCPK:ANCTF) is trading at a very similar multiple.

Conclusion

I still see an intact investment case for Murphy. The company is aggressively buying back shares, and the entire business is likely to have a low single-digit revenue growth rate in the future. Many naturally wonder why a company with such low sales growth would be interesting. However, thanks to aggressive share buybacks, the company's EPS growth remains extremely strong, even though its sales growth is slow.

To illustrate, here are the two CAGRs for revenue and diluted EPS, to better understand the effect of buybacks. After all, stock prices often follow EPS development in the long term.

  • Revenue 3Y: 1.45%
  • Revenue 5Y: 7.69%
  • Revenue 10Y: 2.47%
  • EPS 3Y: 7.74%
  • EPS 5Y: 25.58%
  • EPS 10Y: 15.61%

Therefore, it is likely that EPS will still increase by a double-digit amount each year, even if Murphy's revenues only grow by 0-2% annually in the coming years. Therefore, I continue to view Murphy's stock as attractive, as the shareholder-friendly management is a huge success factor, and I don't see any signs that the stock buybacks will stop.

data.title: Remote Work, IoT, And Stock Repurchases Make Cable One A Buy
data.description:
Cable One, Inc. (NYSE:CABO) could receive more demand for its services after the deployment of the DOCSIS 4.0 along with the emergence of remote work and the IoT industry. Besides, in my opinion, the recent decrease in contract termination costs and potential decrease in taxes in the United States could represent stock price catalysts. Considering ongoing stock repurchases and the company's FCF generation, CABO appears to be trading substantially undervalued.

Cable One Trades At 1.32x FWD Cash Flow

Cable One, Inc. presents itself as a broadband communications company, offering services to more than 1 million residential and business customers in the United States.

The company operates in markets that are expected to grow in the coming years. According to market experts, the broadband services market is expected to grow at about 9.6% CAGR from 2024 to 2032. In addition, the television market size could grow at about 7.7% from 2025 to 2034. I used some of these assumptions in my financial model.

The Broadband Services Market, valued at USD 445.3 billion in 2023, is anticipated to reach USD 1,016.9 billion by 2032, expanding at a CAGR of 9.63% between 2024 and 2032. Source: Broadband Services Market.

Television Market Size was estimated at 396.88 (USD Billion) in 2024. Television Industry is expected to grow from 427.56 (USD Billion) in 2025 to 835.80 (USD Billion) by 2034. The Television Market CAGR (growth rate) is expected to be around 7.7% during the forecast period (2025 - 2034). Source: Television Market.

In the last 10-Q, the company did not report better than expected EPS GAAP. The EPS was close to $0.46, with quarterly revenue of $380 million. In the new quarter, the market expects EPS GAAP of $7.42 per share, and quarterly revenue of $380 million.

In 2025, investors are expecting significant EPS growth of 665%. The company appears to trade at 4.8x 2025 EPS, and 4.5x 2027 EPS. In my view, CABO trades undervalued at close to 1.32x FWD cash flow, and 5x FWD EBITDA. Peers trade at 7x FWD cash flow, and 8x FWD EBITDA.

Higher Speed, And DOCSIS 4.0

In my opinion, CABO will most likely offer better services thanks to higher access speeds. In the last quarter, the company noted that average monthly revenue per unit is usually associated with the company's Wi-Fi offerings, data usage, and broadband capacity. Given the company's recent investments to enhance fiber density, coverage, and network reliability, I believe that we could see further user growth and revenue growth in the coming years.

We continue to invest capital to, among other things, increase fiber density and coverage, expand our footprint, increase plant and data capacity, enhance network reliability and improve the customer experience. We have rolled out multi-Gigabit download data service to over 40% of our markets and currently offer Gigabit download data service to all of our passings. Source: 10-Q.

CABO also reported the deployment of DOCSIS 4.0, which is also expected to grow the company's revenue from business data and residential data, the company's most relevant sources of revenue. In this regard, CABO provided the following commentary in the last quarterly report.

We have also deployed DOCSIS 3.1 and begun the deployment of DOCSIS 4.0, which, together with Sparklight TV, further increases our network capacity and enables future growth in our residential data and business data product lines. Source: 10-Q.

Remote Work, And IoT

Among the reasons to expect user growth in the coming years, there is the growth in the number of individuals working from home.

According to Upwork, by 2025, an estimated 32.6 million Americans will be working remotely, which equates to about 22% of the workforce. Source: Forbes.

Remote work increased during the pandemic, and many individuals tasted the benefit of working from home. In my opinion, many others will try to work in the future, which could accelerate the demand for services offered by CABO.

In addition, I believe that the growth and recent investments in IoT could also improve the demand for broadband communications, WiFi, and other services offered by CABO. According to market experts, the IoT market is expected to grow at close to 24% CAGR from 2024 to 2032. CABO will most likely benefit from this new emerging market.

The global Internet of Things market size was valued at USD 595.73 billion in 2023. The market is projected to grow from USD 714.48 billion in 2024 to USD 4,062.34 billion by 2032, exhibiting a CAGR of 24.3% during the forecast period. Source: Internet of Things Market Size, Share, Growth, Trends, 2032.

Severance And Contract Termination Costs Declined In The Last Quarter

In the last quarter, CABO reported a large decrease in contract termination costs, which means that the number of employees will most likely not decrease in the coming quarters. In my view, the news is quite ideal. It means that directors inside CABO are expecting new projects. They need employees. With this in mind, we could expect revenue growth to remain positive in the future. I made such an assumption in my financial model.

Cable One Will Most Likely Benefit From Decreases In The Total Amount Of Taxes In The United States

For many years, CABO reported positive net income, and paid millions in taxes in the United States. With this in mind and recent information about potential decreases in taxes in the United States, in my opinion, the company is well positioned to benefit from new regulations.

As soon as new information about tax decreases arrives, I believe that analysts will most likely increase future free cash flow growth expectations. As a result, we could expect increases in the demand for the stock and perhaps stock price increases.

Stock Repurchases Could Bring Demand For The Stock

CABO has a share repurchase program, which initially included $450.0 million of share repurchases. In the last quarter, the company reported that it can acquire shares worth up to $143.1 million. Considering that CABO acquired a significant number of shares in the past and the current valuation of the stock, I would expect new stock repurchases at the current price mark.

The Company's Board authorized up to $450.0 million of share repurchases (with no cap as to the number of shares of common stock) under the Share Repurchase Program, which was announced on May 23, 2022. The authorization does not have an expiration date. The Company had $143.1 million of remaining share repurchase authorization under the Share Repurchase Program as of March 31, 2025. Source: 10-Q.

In the past, the company acquired 646,244 shares at close to $556.9 million, which means that CABO acquired shares at a price of more than $800 per share. Right now, we can buy shares in the market at less than $180 per share. It appears quite ideal.

Since the Company first became publicly traded in 2015 through March 31, 2025, the Company has repurchased 646,244 shares of its common stock at an aggregate cost of $556.9 million. The Company did not repurchase any of its common stock during the three months ended March 31, 2025 or 2024. Source: 10-Q.

Debt, And WACC

CABO appears to report a large amount of debt. However, I believe that the company knows well how to negotiate with debt holders out there. According to debt agreements that I could consult, some of the company's debt is linked to changes in the Secured Overnight Financing Rate plus a margin of about 1.25% to 1.75%. Given the company's debt agreements, I assumed a WACC of about 7%, which I believe is conservative. The following text about several debt instruments signed by CABO has been taken from the last quarterly report.

Under the Credit Agreement, the interest margins applicable to the Senior Credit Facilities are, at the Company's option, equal to either the Secured Overnight Financing Rate ("SOFR") or a base rate, plus an applicable margin equal to, (i) with respect to the Revolving Credit Facility, 1.25% to 1.75% plus a 10 basis point credit spread adjustment for SOFR loans and 0.25% to 0.75% for base rate loans, Source: 10-Q.

Risks

In my view, the largest risk for CABO comes from the total amount of debt and future negotiations with debt holders. In my opinion, debt holders may impose certain restrictions on the company in the form of covenants and other agreements. As a result, CABO may not be able to sign M&A agreements, or make large investments in new infrastructure. Under the worst-case scenario, CABO may not receive sufficient financing to further develop new networks, and revenue growth may be lower than expected.

Besides, I believe that increases in the interest rates could also lower the company's potential for net income growth and free cash flow growth. As a result, the company's net debt/EBITDA ratio could increase substantially, which may also lower the interest of certain investors. If the demand for the stock decreases, I would expect a decline in the price. In this regard, it is worth noting that CABO signed interest rate swap agreements to protect the company from large changes in the interest rates.

The Company is party to two interest rate swap agreements, designated as cash flow hedges, to manage the risk of fluctuations in interest rates on its variable rate SOFR debt. Source: 10-Q.

CABO operates in an industry that is regulated by the Federal Communications Commission and other entities. In the future, the FCC could block M&A deals, change the market share that players can control all over the country, and make decisions that may harm CABO's business model. I am not in a position to say whether the new regulations could benefit CABO, so I considered that new information about changes in the industry represents risks for the company.

Valuation

In the past, the company reported FCF of $400-$500 million. Hence, my free cash flow expectations include free cash flow of about $409 million in 2026, $550 million in 2031, and $662 million in 2035. I also included assumptions about future remote work, and the development of the IoT industry.

In addition, if we also include terminal FCF of 2.5x, net debt of $3.3 billion, 5.6 million shares, and a WACC of 7%, the implied valuation would stand at $195 per share.

My Final Words: Cabo Is A Buy At 1.32x FWD Cash Flow

CABO will most likely see an increase in its user base thanks to the deployment of DOCSIS 4.0 and further development of infrastructure to offer high-speed communications. I also believe that further expansion of remote workers and new development with regard to the IoT will most likely accelerate the demand for CABO's services. Besides, I believe that the recent decrease in contract termination costs, the potential decrease in taxes in the United States, and ongoing stock repurchases are good reasons to follow CABO. The company is clearly trading at very undervalued price marks. From here, CABO looks like a buy at 1.32x FWD cash flow.

data.title: Hims & Hers: Outsized Subscription Growth Is Enviable - Premium Valuations
data.description:
HIMS's Recovery Occurred Overly Fast & Furious - Reversing Momentum Likely Ahead

We previously covered Hims & Hers Health, Inc. (NYSE:HIMS) in March 2025, discussing how its steep correction had been well warranted, given the expensive valuations and the headwinds from the now available FDA-approved GLP-1 therapies.

While we had believed that its core personalized healthcare portfolio was likely to continue outperforming, as observed in the double digits non-GLP-1 revenue growth, the rich FY2024 performance metrics, and the management's promising FY2025 guidance, the stock's overly expensive valuations were undeniable then, worsened by the drastically higher short interest volume by +242.3% on a YoY basis.

HIMS 1Y Stock Price

Since that reiterated Hold rating, HIMS has further retraced deeply by -37% nearer to our fair value estimates of $25.50 offered in our prior article indeed, thanks to the market wide meltdown in April 2025 with a similar pullback also observed in its obesity therapy/ GLP-1 peers in varying degrees.

Much of the headwinds are naturally attributed to the regulatory scrutiny observed in the healthcare provider/ insurance sector, worsened by the potential impacts from the "President Trump's new Executive Order that aims to drastically lower prescription drug prices for consumers."

This is on top of the ongoing Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services' pricing negotiations for Novo Nordisk's (NVO) Wegovy, Ozempic, and Rybelsus since early January 2025, as the new US administration also opts to not endorse the future Medicare coverage for obesity drugs as of early April 2025, with it naturally triggering headwinds to the GLP-1 sector as a whole.

On the other hand, thanks to the excellent dollar cost averaging opportunity, we are unsurprised that the bulls have already defended HIMS' floor at the $25s ranges during the worst of the April 2025 correction, as the stock rallies by +133.6% to retest the prior February heights in the $59s range by the time of writing.

Much of its tailwinds are naturally attributed to the increased availability of NVO's and Eli Lilly’s (LLY) GLP-1 offerings on the HIMS platform, as the latter continues to offer compounded formulations for "patients that are suffering from some type of intolerable dynamic where a commercial available dose is just not actually an option for them."

These naturally allow them to achieve the best of both worlds in GLP-1 related growth while complying with the "personalized 503A (regulation), ... we're talking about personalization of the [A facilities] (ph), that is regulatorily protected. So I think there's an alignment that both the regulation allows for."

As a result of these developments, we are unsurprised by HIM's double beat FQ1'25 performance, with total revenues of $586.01M (+21.7% QoQ/ +110.6% YoY), adj EBITDA margins of 15.5% (+4.3 points QoQ/ +3.9 YoY/ +95.5 from FY2019 levels of -80%), and GAAP EPS of $0.20 (+81.8% QoQ/ +300% YoY).

Most importantly, its unique D2C and subscription approaches have paid off handsomely, as observed in the higher online revenues of $576.36M (+22.4% QoQ/ +115.2% YoY) thanks to the growing subscribers of 2.36M (+6.3% QoQ/ +38.8% YoY) and the expanding Monthly Online Revenue per Average Subscriber at $84 (+15% QoQ/ +52.7% YoY).

With 59.3% of its subscribers adopting the personalized solutions (+4.3 points QoQ/ +24.3 YoY), it is unsurprising that HIMS has rallied as it has, attributed to its ability to differentiate itself from other telehealth and legacy healthcare providers through the personalized solutions.

This is on top of the integration of its new laboratory diagnostics/ preventative testing, the enhanced fulfillment capabilities/ infrastructure, the expanded specialty offerings across obesity, trichology, sexual health (men/ female), mental health, and dermatology, and others:

Our vision involves expanding from hundreds of personalized treatments today to potentially thousands, powered by richer insights from lab diagnostics, growing subscriber data sets, and eventually daily tracking from wearable devices. (Seeking Alpha)

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These reasons may also be why HIMS has felt confident to raise their FY2025 guidance, with revenues of $2.35B at the midpoint (+59.8% YoY) and adj EBITDA margins of 13.4% (+1.5 points YoY), with it building upon the robust FY2024 growth profile at +68.9% YoY and 11.9% (+6.3 points YoY), respectively.

This is on top of the healthcare company's ambitious 2030 target, with revenues of $6.5B (expanding at a CAGR of +28.1% from FY2024 levels) and adj EBITDA of $1.3B (expanding at a CAGR of +40.3%).

These reasons may also be why the consensus have already raised their forward estimates, with HIMS expected to chart an accelerated top/ bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +32%/ +34.4% through FY2027.

This is compared to the prior estimates of +14.7%/ +5.9% (a year ago), while building upon the 5Y historical top-line growth at a CAGR of +78%.

This is on top of the richer Free Cash Flow generation, the healthier balance sheet with a growing net cash position of $322.66M (+7.4% QoQ/ +58.4% YoY/ +434.5% from FY2019 levels of $60.36M), and the inherent lack of debts, with it underscoring how the growing adoption of HIMS' personalized healthcare offerings/ subscriptions have driven its growth opportunities.

HIMS Valuations

These reasons may also be why the market has upgraded HIMS' valuations to FWD EV/ EBITDA valuations of 41.13x and FWD P/E GAAP valuations to 85.99x, up from the recent bottom of 17.93x/ 37.90x, the 1Y mean of 27.32x/ 111.77x, and the 3Y mean of 29.16x/ NA, respectively.

The notable premium compared to the sector median of 11.90x/ 23.97x, respectively, implies that the stock's recent recovery has occurred overly fast and furious, especially when compared to its primary care legacy/ telehealth/ GLP-1 peers, including:

  • CVS Health Corporation (CVS) at FWD EV/ EBITDA of 9.59x,
  • Walgreens Boots Alliance, Inc. (WBA) at 11.45x,
  • Teladoc Health, Inc. (TDOC) at 5.78x,
  • UpHealth, Inc. (UPH) at 12.84x,
  • NVO at 13.72x, and
  • LLY at 29.03x.

This development implies that a moderate pullback to our Buy zones may be necessary for an improved return profile, one that we shall further discuss in the next segment.

So, Is HIMS Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

HIMS 5Y Stock Price

For now, HIMS has already returned to its February 2025 heights as the stock runs away from its 50/ 100/ 200 day moving averages, albeit failing to break out of its prior resistance levels of $59s ranges.

Based on the management's raised FY2025 adj EBITDA guidance to $315M (+78% YoY) and the last reported shares outstanding of 246.61M, we are looking at an estimated FY2025 adj EBITDA of $1.27 per share (+73.9% YoY).

Combined with the higher 3Y EV/ EBITDA mean of 29.16x (previously at 20.91x), it is apparent that HIMS remains extremely expensive to our updated fair value estimates of $37.00.

Despite the raised consensus FY2027 adj EBITDA estimates to $523.48M and the resultant FY2027 adj EBITDA of $2.12 per share (expanding at a 2Y CAGR of +29.2%), it is undeniable that there remains a minimal margin of safety to our updated long-term price target of $61.80.

HIMS' Overbought Levels

The same overbought position has also been observed in HIMS' technical metrics, with the stock's inability to break out of the $59s ranges and the historical trends implying an ongoing reversal in its price trends (red oval).

Assuming that its historical trading trends persist, we are likely to see the stock retrace to its 100/ 200 day moving averages of between $42.50 and $32.80 in the intermediate term, with it implying a -36.4% downside at the midpoint from current levels.

The potential for volatility is observed in the drastically higher short interest volumes by +187.7% on a YoY basis as well, with any moderation in market sentiments from prior greed levels likely trigger a deep retracement from current levels.

As a result of the potential capital losses at current heights, we prefer to maintain our Hold rating for the HIMS stock.

Interested investors may want to wait for our Buy zones between the 100/ 200 day moving averages to materialize for an improved margin of safety. Despite the bright long-term prospects, some patience may be more prudent in the meantime.

data.title: Iberdrola: Stronger Conviction That This Is An Attractive Investment
data.description:
Summary

Following my coverage on Iberdrola (OTCPK:IBDSF) in July 2024, in which I recommended a buy rating as I envisioned the world demanding way more power than it does today, and that will be a great demand tailwind for IBDSF, this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock. I am now even more confident in the equity story. IBDSF is no longer just an energy producer, it is gradually becoming a low-volatility, infrastructure CAPEX-driven earnings grower.

Networks Become the Core Growth Engine

I have pointed out previously that IBDSF’s network business is a beneficiary of Europe’s grid modernization push, and I believe that narrative has become even more important since July last year. For a start, the 1Q25 results say it all: the regulated networks segment’s EBITDA grew 42.8% y/y to EUR2.42 billion and accounted for 52% of total group EBITDA. This is a very important shift that gives more confidence in the IBDSF earnings growth outlook as it fundamentally upgrades the quality of the IBDSF earnings base. As a recap, regulated networks operate under cost-plus tariff regimes; in exchange for this regulation, IBDSF has regional monopolies with predictable, inflation-linked returns that are not impacted by commodity price cycles.

The shift in earnings quality has a much more important implication than just earnings visibility. It also reduces the consolidated volatility in earnings from swings in power prices and renewables output (i.e., the generation and supply segment). More importantly, it significantly de-risks the equity story, as IBDSF is now seen as a much more stable company with visible earnings growth, and I think this justifies the case for a premium valuation multiple.

CAPEX Translates into Visible Earnings Growth

One of the risks I mentioned previously was that high capex needs may be a possible headwind for free cash flow (FCF). I no longer think that way. Management noted in the recent results that they plan to invest >EUR13 billion in networks between 2025 and 2026, and this is the important part: 90% of this is expected to be cash-generative within the first year. This is a very critical distinction, as it means this capex starts contributing to returns almost immediately, rather than years down the line (which is where the FCF gap may appear). As such, my refreshed view is that I believe IBDSF should invest aggressively in CAPEX because there is high visibility to the timing of returns, and it makes a lot of sense to win the deal because it ensures a regional monopoly for a long time.

As of 1Q25, IBDSF's regulated asset base has climbed to EUR49 billion and is expected to exceed EUR51 billion by the end of this year. This growing network base sets up a very positive stage for the network segment to become an even larger mix of the business, and importantly, rather than a drag, CAPEX is now the clearest driver of earnings growth. This dynamic should also support a premium valuation multiple.

Spanish Blackout Provides an Unexpected Regulatory Catalyst

I don’t mean to frame this as a positive thing (for the country and population), but I believe the major blackout in Spain in April 2025 is a positive for IBDSF’s business and stock. This has given a strong pretext for politicians to focus on grid resilience, which I expect to significantly speed up the timing for grid investments (note: grid investments were typically slow-moving). This creates a strong setup for IBDSF as it heads into Spain’s 2026–2031 regulatory review. My view is that the odds of a more supportive tariff framework (this could potentially allow higher returns) have increased. For investors, this is a solid catalyst that could unlock accelerated earnings growth from IBDSF's domestic networks business.

Disciplined Approach to Renewables

While the growth story is now anchored in the networks segment, that is not to say the IBDSF generation & supply segment is weak. It is also an important driver of earnings. In fact, I think the quality of this earnings stream has also gotten better since last year. I used to think that this was just another way for IBDSF to capture rising power demand. That narrative still stays, but the risk associated has been lowered. On the 1Q25 call, management disclosed two critical data points that led me to this conclusion:

  1. >4 TWh per year of new Power Purchase Agreements (PPAs) have been signed in the past 12 months.
  2. 85% of investment in new renewable capacity is either backed by long-term PPAs or government-secured Contracts for Difference.

As such, this earnings stream quality has structurally improved from one that is dependent on market wholesale prices to one that has predictable profitable prices. This basically transforms what might otherwise be considered volatile cash flow into predictable, bond-like cash flows.

Combined with the stability of the Networks business, I believe IBDSF has significantly enhanced the defensiveness of its overall portfolio.

Valuation

I believe the upside has gotten even more attractive as we can now forecast earnings further out into the future (because of the structural improvement in earnings quality). My model assumes IBDSF continues to grow adj. EBITDA at high-single-digit percentages (~9%) through FY28, exiting FY28 to EUR23.6 billion. As I said above, a fundamental improvement in earnings quality justifies a higher multiple. Assuming multiple shifts up to 11x (it's 10.6x now), I see very attractive upsides from here.

Risk

Spain’s 2026–2031 regulatory review may turn out to be a negative outcome, and that could weigh on returns for the Networks business. IBDSF's global footprint also means it is heavily impacted by FX movements. While the pivot to regulated assets improves earnings quality, a sizeable chunk of IBDSF earnings is still exposed to merchant risk.

Conclusion

In conclusion, my rating for IBDSF is a buy. The earnings quality has improved and with network capex yielding fast and visible returns, a growing regulated asset base, improved renewables contract structure, and a supportive regulatory backdrop post-Spanish blackout, I believe IBDSF is an attractive long.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: UnitedHealth: Diversity Is The Key To Hedging Against Looming Challenges
data.description:
Thesis statement

I am bullish on (NYSE:UNH), driven by the company’s diversified model seen in an impressive FY2024 growth outlook of between $450 billion and $455 billion for FY2025. Despite projected federal Medicaid budget cuts between 2025 and 2026, the company maintains its profitability and new clients. The diversification of revenue streams primarily drives this increase in revenues amidst a volatile healthcare sector. Due to projected federal Medicaid expenditure cuts of around $880 billion over 10 years, which would result in a 12% reduction in Medicaid spending to a total of $7.5 trillion over the same period, the impacts on reimbursements will be felt by providers such as UnitedHealth as seen in Optum Health. For instance, the Q1’2025 revenues of the Optum Health segment were affected by the rise in new patients, reporting $63.9 billion from $65.1 billion in Q4’2024. However, the segment expects to serve over 650,000 value-based patients in 2025. The most crucial point here is to note that as the projected Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) continue to experience budget cuts, the rise in low reimbursement patients risks the company’s revenue projection. So far, it is evident that there is a risk associated with the expansion in the customer base of the low-reimbursement category of Medicare Advantage. Here is where I bring up my bullish angle by looking at the diversified portfolio that has grown its revenues in Q1’2025 by 8.7% from Q4’2024 revenues of $100.8 billion to $109.6 billion Q1’2025. The diversification is UnitedHealth strong basis of the company’s bullish angle. For example, in the first quarter of 2025, the company benefits for self-funded clients rose by 700,000, which shows a strong uptake in the company’s innovative offerings such as modern and high-performing systems serving over 148 million people. I will explain further how a diversified portfolio leads to a bullish rating in my explanations below.

Company Brief

UnitedHealth Group is a healthcare company that offers its services in the United States and internationally. The company operates through four segments: UnitedHealthcare, Optum Rx, Optum Health, and Optum Insight. All these segments offer a specific type of service. For instance, the UnitedHealthcare segment offers consumer-oriented health benefits such as Medicaid plans that take care of economically disadvantaged people. Optum Health offers services to various entities, such as care delivery systems, payers, and public-sector entities. Optimum Insight provides services such as information and software products, while Optum RX is a pharmacy-related segment. This segment provides services such as infusion, clinical and purchasing capabilities, and drug therapy management, among others. The company started its operations in 1974 when it was incorporated and headquartered in Eden Prince, Minnesota.

Bullish Perspective

At the height of the largest Medicaid budget cut in the history of the US Federal health insurance, this exposes a minimum of 8.6 million Americans without health insurance. While this budget seeks to slash about a trillion dollars in health and social services expenditure in about a 10-year period, over 80 million people relying on this scheme from Medicaid are about to face difficulties accessing quality and affordable healthcare. Most of the beneficiaries under Medicaid in the 80 million people category are working families, seniors, children, low-income adults, and people with disabilities. Given that Americans in this category are likely to be affected the most, it is projected that this is likely to increase the loss of life between 651 and 12,626 due to the inaccessibility of healthcare for preventable demises.

When I look at these possible outcomes of Medicaid budget cuts, I believe that UnitedHealth Group has a bigger opportunity than a loss. What makes me draw this conclusion is that the company is recording improved subscriptions in its various segments, which invokes my opportunistic angle. Let’s look at a segment like UnitedHealthcare. This segment’s self-funded commercial benefits improved by 700,000 in the first quarter, driven by the high-priced competitive market. Additionally, the company’s services to seniors and people with sophisticated needs grew by 545,000 in the first quarter of 2025, and this number is projected to hit 800,000 by the end of 2025. In my opinion, this improvement in subscriptions is driven by the rising cost of healthcare and increased uncertainty of the Affordable Care Act 2010 (ACA). The ACA subsidies created under the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) nears expiration, leaving over 4 million people at risk of facing expensive covers. These subsidies are not likely to be renewed, and their expiration in 2025 is what makes Americans seek more private insurance coverage.

Note that these Medicaid budget cuts expose private insurance companies to reimbursement reductions. For instance, when UnitedHealth Group cites that Medicaid funding reductions resulted in a revenue decline of the Optum Health segment in Q1’2025, this opens a blueprint of how the inevitable revenue reductions could hit providers. However, I cover this part to show how UnitedHealth Group also faces this challenge but also shows that its diversified portfolio makes it resilient. Given that 54% of Americans use Medicare, the number of Americans using the private Medicare Advantage scheme has increased from 8 million to 33 million from 2007 to 2024.

Therefore, from my bullish stance, I am looking at UnitedHealth’s diversified model as its strategic positioning to offer services to patients with complex illnesses, low-income earners, and seniors. In Q’2025, UnitedHealthcare consumers grew by 780,000 from 2.4 million in Q4’2025 to 3.2 million, reflecting a good consumer uptake of its innovative offerings. These increases in customers are reflected in the company segment revenue growth in UnitedHealthcare, Optum Insight, and Optum Rx in Q1’2025. See my explanations on financials below;

Financials

The total revenues in Q1’2025 increased by 8.74% YoY from $100.8 billion in Q1’2024 to $109.6 billion. Earnings from operations also saw a 16.7% increase YoY from $7.8 billion in Q1’2024 to $9.1 billion in Q1’2024. This delivered improved net earnings by 14.5% at $6.3 billion in Q1’2025 from $5.5 billion in Q1’2024. I also note that the company improved its operational efficiency from an improved operating margin of 7.7% in Q1’2024 to 8.3% in Q1’2025. Cash flow from operations dropped from $24.2 billion in Q4’2024 to $5.4 billion in Q1’2025. However, I believe the decline of cash flow from operations in the quarter is due to a change in the cycle from Q4’2024 to Q1’2025. Cash from operations remains an area to watch, but given the revenue improvements YoY and more customer subscriptions, I reiterate my bullish angle.

UnitedHealthcare revenues rose by 12.3% from $75.357 billion in Q1’2024 to $84.617 billion in Q1’2025, driven primarily by senior care and self-funded consumers. Optum Insight rose by 2.8% from $4.502 billion in Q1’2024 to $4.630 billion in Q1’2025. Optum Rx grew by 13.9% from $30.835 billion in Q1’2024 to $35.132 billion in Q1’2025, driven by growth in adjusted scripts for new and existing clients. I believe that these increases in new members show UnitedHealth Group’s strategic positioning in the increasing private Medicare Advantage customers.

Let’s look at the balance sheet. I evaluate the debt-to-equity ratio to determine its debt situation by taking the recent FY2024 total debt. With a total debt of $76,904 million and a total equity of $98,268 million, the company’s debt-to-equity ratio is 0.78. Compared to the industry average debt-to-equity of 0.6264, the company debt is well managed.

Also, from the debt-to-asset perspective, at total assets of $298,278 million and total debt of $76,904 million, the company debt-to-asset ratio stands at 0.26 or 26%. This is below the industry average of 47.65%, which shows the company does not rely heavily on its debt to fund its assets. It then follows that UnitedHealth Group has low debt risk and has a substantial cushion in case of a downturn.

On debt repayment, the company is on strong ground, its interest coverage given by its trailing EBITDA of $36,238 million and interest expense at $4,060 million is at 8.9. This shows that it has a healthy interest coverage ratio, which is an indication that its earnings can cover its debt obligations adequately, hence a strong financial position and minimal debt risk.

Valuation

I will value this company using the PE ratio and the EV/EBITDA ratio in relation to its competitors Elevance Health, Inc (NYSE:ELV) and Humana Inc (NYSE:HUM). I believe these multiples reflect the company’s financial strength. From the PE perspective, UNH is trading at 12.9x, which is more than 20% lower than its peers’ average of 16.29x. This implies that this stock is undervalued.

Surprisingly, its lower valuation comes at a time when it not only has the highest net margin of the three companies but also when it has improved its margin significantly in the recent past better than its peers. In my view, based on this phenomenon, I think UNH warrants a premium valuation relative to its counterparts. I think a PE in the region of 18x is justified given this company’s stronger profitability.

Similarly, from the EV/EBITDA ratio, UNH 9.36x falls short of its peers' multiple, another indication that this stock is trading at a discount.

Surprisingly, this discounted value is far from justifiable given this company’s unmatched EBITDA margin of 8.84% which stands almost 2x that of its close competitor at 5.37%.

Given these multiples, I think the market is mispricing UNH. Given its diverse business model, which, I think, could mitigate the current headwinds, I think investors should seize this cheap entry point. Assuming a conservative PE of 16x and the trailing diluted EPS of $23.82, I estimate a fair value of about $381.12 which translates to an upside potential of approximately 24%. My estimate is within range of the average consensus price target of 25 analysts, which stands at $426.52.

Investment risks

Competition – There are risks of competitors taking more market share of private Medicare Advantage at this time when there is a shift in federal healthcare insurance financial reduction.

Regulatory Changes – there is a risk of potential regulatory changes in case more Americans face difficulties in affording insurance coverage due to the termination of subsidies under the IRA. This risks UnitedHealth Group’s revenues from low-income earners.

Risk from rising healthcare costs – When subsidies end in 2025 and healthcare reduction continues taking effect, the insurance providers face a risk of low reimbursements. Federal programs may lead to the inability to conduct business effectively due to the unaffordability of covers.

Conclusion

I am opportunistic about this company because I look at it from an angle of market repositioning as the health insurance crisis looms following healthcare reductions. The company’s diversity is its major strength in taking business from patients with complex health issues, seniors, and affordable covers.

data.title: Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Week 224: $22,400 Allocated, $2,349.12 In Projected Dividends
data.description:
While it feels like things are going well, this was a tough week as the S&P 500 fell -0.71% while the Nasdaq declined by -0.98%. The S&P 500 had closed above 6,000 on Friday, June 9th, and started the week higher on June 9th as investors awaited updates from the U.S. and China trade talks. The optimism faded at the end of the week as the markets faced pressure from escalating Middle East tensions after Israel's attack on Iran. The latest CPI report, which was released on Wednesday, June 11th, showed that inflation came in at 2.4%, which was slightly up from 2.3% in April, while Core CPI came in at 2.8%, which was below the consensus estimates of 2.9%. We also got the PPI report, which came in below expectations at 2.6%. The FOMC meeting is next week, and I don't believe that the Fed is going to cut rates, even though they should. With the earnings season winding down, the only positive news for the next few weeks will be if new trade deals are announced and if geopolitical headwinds subside. I'm looking forward to allocating capital throughout many different positions heading into the summer and believe there are still many opportunities in the market despite the S&P 500 getting close to all-time highs again.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio showed a lot of resilience this week as the markets headed lower. After allocating $22,400 to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio, the account balance finished week 224 at $28,427.70. The account's profitability increased 0.30% ($18.08) to $6,027.70, which is a return on invested capital of 26.91%. This week, I added to VICI Properties (VICI), NNN REIT (NNN), the BlackRock Science and Technology Term Trust (BSTZ), and the YieldMax MSTR Option Income Strategy ETF (MSTY). The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $29.79 of dividend income in week 224. The combination of reinvesting the generated dividend income and making these investments increased the forward projected annualized dividend income by $38.35 (1.66%) to $2,349.12. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has generated $4,198 of income since inception, which is 18.74% of the total capital I have allocated toward the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. I don't believe that the CPI or PPI data that we received will impact the Fed's decision, and they will end up being notoriously late cutting rates, the same way they were behind the 8-ball when they started hiking. This will allow investors such as myself to allocate capital toward companies such as VICI and NNN before they catch a bid when the Fed does start cutting, as it will improve their cost of capital. It's going to be interesting to see what Fed Chair Powell says, and I am excited to continue allocating capital toward the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio.

The Overall Performance of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio

Weeks like this show why the level of diversification I have generated can become extremely helpful. The market declined by -0.71%, and the profitability of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio increased by 0.3%. I started this account with $100 and have allocated $100 each week since its inception to document how I would build out a new portfolio from the ground up on a fixed budget. Many people would leave comments on my articles about how income investing doesn't work unless you have a tremendous amount of capital to start with, which I believe I am proving to be an incorrect investment theory. After allocating $22,400, the account balance is $28,427.70, which is a return of 26.91% ($6,027.70) on invested capital, while the portfolio is expected to generate $2,349.12 in forward dividend income. My investment goals have been to mitigate downside risk while generating continuous income. Capital appreciation has never been the main focus of this account, as it has been a secondary objective. I limit my exposure to a single position to 5% and 20% per sector. I am happy with the performance of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio and feel that my strategy is delivering on all my investment goals.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Dividend Section

Here's how much dividend income is generated per investment basket:

  • ETFs $769.92 (32.77%)
  • Equities $554.07 (23.59%)
  • CEFs $400.48 (17.05%)
  • REITs $381.13 (16.22%)
  • BDCs $243.51 (10.37%)

Collecting dividends can serve many functions in a portfolio. Some investors utilize dividends to supplement their income and live off of them. I'm building a dividend portfolio for myself 30 years into the future. In 2022, I collected $507.80 in dividend income from 533 dividends. In 2023, I collected $978.11 in dividend income from 660 dividends. 2024 was a great year as the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $1,623.14 in dividend income from 709 dividends. After 23 weeks in 2025, $988.83 of dividend income was produced, which is 60.92% of the total income from 2024. There were 327 positions that paid a dividend, which is 46.12% of the dividends generated in 2024.

In week 24 of last year, the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $21.73 in dividend income. This week I generated $29.79, which is an increase of 37.09% ($8.06) YoY. This has brought my weekly dividend income average to $42.44, which is an increase of 35.97% ($11.23) YoY. 2025 is looking much different than 2024, as I have already generated 62.76% of the income in less than half the year. Based on the current trajectory of the income that's being produced, I think it's entirely possible that the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio will exceed $2,500 in forward dividend income by the end of 2025, and $125 will exceed the new high on weekly income.

June is coming in strong, as I have generated 74.21% of the dividend income from last year in just 2-weeks. There is just over 2 weeks left this month, and I have a feeling that I am going to exceed $200 of dividend income this month. So far, only January has exceeded $200, and I am excited to see how the first half of 2025 closes out. Once June is over, I will revise my projections, but I have a feeling that I will exceed $200 in dividend income during June or July and start setting new trends going forward.

This week BP p.l.c. (BP) fell off the list of companies that are generating at least 1 share from their dividends on an annual basis. There are now 44 companies accomplishing this goal. I added to NNN and VICI this week, as I am continuing to try and get more companies into the green section of the table below. The new shares from the 44 companies that are generating at least 1 share per year are expected to add $358.65 in forward dividend income. I believe that I can get at least 5–10 companies to cross over into the green section by the end of 2025.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Composition

I need to face the fact that ETFs and REITs will remain the largest sectors for a long time in the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. There is a lot of opportunity in REITs, and I have a feeling capital will be flowing toward them over the next several months. Individual equities now represent 38.71% of the Dividend Harvesting portfolio while generating 23.59% of the dividend income. REITs, ETFs, CEFs, and BDCs make up 61.29% of the portfolio and generate 76.41% of the forward income. I am working on getting individual equities to represent more of the portfolio. The long-term goal is to have the sectors be close to equal weight, but that may not happen for 5–10 years at this rate, if ever. I have been adding to energy companies as I believe there is a big opportunity there, and I will probably continue allocating capital toward energy positions as time progresses.

Altria Group (MO) is now 4.51% of the portfolio as it continues to represent less and less of the total account value. For a long time, this was hovering around 5%, and I am happy that it's starting to really recede. While I have tried to allocate capital away from the top-5 positions, I may need to add to Enbridge (ENB) this week because it has paid a few dividends, and since they don't reinvest, it's around that time for me to add another share. My goal is to have all of them represent less than 3.5% of the portfolio. I think that the top-20 positions are going to change a bit over the next several months, as I see a bunch of opportunities I am looking to allocate capital toward the long-term goal.

This week, the NEOS NASDAQ-100(R) High Income ETF (QQQI) rejoined the top-10 positions as it replaced the BlackRock Science and Technology Trust (BST). My allocation to the top 10 positions is $6,109.06, and they finished the week with a value of $8,523.56. This is an ROI of $2,414.50 (39.52%). There has been $1,035.02 of dividends generated and reinvested, which is 16.94% of the initial investment into these positions. I'm now projecting that $595.71 of dividend income will be generated from the top 10 holdings, which have a forward yield of 9.75%. These positions represent 29.98% of the portfolio, while their forward dividend income is projected to generate 25.36% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio's annualized income.

Week 224 Additions

In week 224, I allocated capital toward VICI Properties, NNN REIT, the BlackRock Science and Technology Term Trust, and the YieldMax MSTR Option Income Strategy ETF.

VICI Properties

  • I recently wrote an article about my investment thesis on VICI (can be read here)
  • VICI Properties offers a compelling mix of income stability and growth, with a 5.5% yield and consistent dividend increases over six years
  • VICI focuses on experiential, high-barrier-to-entry assets, and long-term triple-net leases, specifically in the gaming sector with tenants such as Caesars
  • For the past 10 quarters, VICI has increased its revenue on a QoQ basis and is operating at a 76.76% margin and has a profit margin that exceeds 55%.

NNN REIT

  • I recently wrote an article about my investment thesis on NNN (can be read here)
  • NNN offers stability, high occupancy, and 35 years of dividend growth, making it ideal for income-focused investors seeking passive real estate exposure.
  • I think that when the Fed eventually cuts rates, NNN will dramatically benefit as its cost of capital will decline
  • NNN is generating a dividend yield of around 5.5% with decades of growth behind it, which is appealing to me

BlackRock Science and Technology Term Trust

  • BSTZ trades at a -8.68% discount to its NAV, and it's contingent limited-term structure offers a guaranteed liquidity event at NAV in 2031
  • BSTZ is yielding 13.35%, which is a tremendous yield when I'm getting the book of assets at a discount
  • I wanted to own more BSTZ as it has a large focus on technology with a sizable position in NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA)
  • I think BSTZ will do well in a lower-rate environment

YieldMax MSTR Option Income Strategy ETF

  • I had some capital left over, so I added another share of MSTY
  • I am planning on limiting my investment into any YieldMax ETF in this account to $100
  • MSTY has a huge yield of 137.58% based on the TTM distribution, and when I ran the numbers, I think the opportunity is very compelling

Week 225 Game Plan

Next week I am planning on adding to ENB, and potentially BP and Truist Financial Corporation (TFC).

Conclusion

Things are looking good for the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio as it was able to mitigate downside risk and finish the week in the black while the S&P 500 fell -0.71%. The amount of dividend income being generated and reinvested is impressive, and I am now projected to generate an average of $195.76 per month going forward. My long-term strategy of developing a diversified portfolio that mitigates downside risk while generating recurring income remains effective, and reinvesting the dividends helps grow the future income stream. Sooner or later, I will generate $5,200 of dividend income from this account, which means that the dividends will match the weekly contributions on an annual basis. This portfolio has become a mini-ETF as there are 102 positions, which has helped mitigate downside risk no matter what macroeconomic or geopolitical event has occurred. I look forward to continuing to allocate capital, as I believe there are numerous opportunities in the market for investors to capitalize on. Please leave all of your suggestions in the comment section, as I look forward to interacting with everyone.

data.title: D-Wave Quantum: A Highly Speculative Asymmetrically Risky Bet
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome WaiRua Research as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

D-Wave Quantum Inc. (NYSE:QBTS) offers a highly speculative risk-reward profile within the volatile, high-growth industry of quantum computing. The stock’s recent momentum is less based on improving fundamentals and more on retail-driven speculation, reflected in its continued growing popularity in forums such as r/WallStreetBets. While these short-term momentum-based trades can generate sharp rallies, they often lack the economic and fundamental durability required for long-term compounding.

The true “sniff” test for assessing a company’s long-term portfolio viability by an investor is the ability of the company to withstand economic shocks, measured through the strength of its balance sheet, defensibility of its moat, and consistency of revenues. While D-Wave Quantum is a pioneer in quantum annealing technology, it falls short on all three counts, raising serious concerns for long-term investors.

1. Fragile Financials, Heavy Cash Burn

A review of D-Wave’s FY2024 SEC filing reveals a concerning financial profile. The company reported a net loss of $143m for the year, driven primarily by $82m in operating expenses. This is against a meagre $8.8m in revenue. This translates to a revenue-to-expense ratio of ~0.11. This disproportionate cost structure is reflective of the company’s early-stage positioning and capex and the R&D-intensive nature of quantum computing development. While high cash burn rates are not atypical to deep tech ventures, the scale of the gap presents concerns about the sustainability of operations without continued access to external funding.

The income statement includes a significant non-cash adjustment of -$68m related to “warrant liabilities”, a legacy item from D-Wave’s SPAC merger. As the company’s share price continues to appreciate, the “warrant liabilities” cause a drag on net income due to their mark-to-market accounting treatment. These warrants are available to be exercised by holders upon notice. This adds continued balance sheet volatility.

To reach breakeven, at current levels of expense, D-Wave would need to scale up its revenue from $8.8m to ~144m. This is almost a 16x increase. Achieving this level of revenue growth within the next 3-5 years seems challenging without a substantial acceleration in ARR.

Most of D-Wave’s revenues stem from Quantum Computing as a Service Subscription. In contrast, we notice that the revenues from one-time discovery and professional service engagement declined. There is specifically a decrease in professional service agreements with corporate clients. This is often the initial step of the company’s sales pipeline, where discovery projects are utilised to identify potential, real-world applications for quantum annealing technology within the client’s operations. The intent here by management is to convert discovery outcomes into longer-term ARR through QCaaS Subscriptions.

The balance sheet looks deceptively strong. However, this is due to aggressive capital raises in recent years. As of Q4 FY24, D-Wave Quantum held $177m in cash, up from $41m in the prior year. This is mainly driven by equity issuance. A subsequent $150m at the market offering in February 2025 has taken the total cash balance to $320m. While this provides a short-term runway, a back-hand calculation considering roughly (~82m Opex and ~2m Capex) finds the cash balance lasting approximately 4 years. I would require sustainable revenue generation to gain confidence in the firm's balance sheet.

D-Wave’s cash flow statement shows a temporary uplift from equity issuances, which have helped bolster the company’s short-term liquidity. This, alongside developments of a successful $150m capital raise, has led to roughly ~320m of cash on D-Wave’s pro forma balance sheet. The shoring up of cash is common for early-stage, high-growth tech companies, especially as D-Wave continues to face financial pressures, particularly due to its ongoing investment in R&D initiatives associated with Quantum Annealing technology.

In FY24, the operating cash flow was -$42.6m, alongside capex of $2.4m, which generated a total cash outlay of ~$45m. If we take ~$320m of cash, this implies a theoretical cash burn horizon of 7 years, but this tends to assume static opex/capex and no revenue growth, which are unlikely real-world developments. As the company continues to scale its infrastructure, workforce, and sales capabilities, its expenses will likely rise, alongside higher revenue growth potentially shortening the horizon.

To estimate breakeven, I model with the following assumptions:

  • Model revenue growth at 50% CAGR, consistent with industry trends of early-stage quantum computing firms and reflective of the small FY24 revenue base ($8.8m).
  • Opex is expected to grow at 10%, aligning with a growing R&D cost base, and additional commercial operations.
  • Capex is assumed to grow at 5%, in line with D-Wave’s largely cloud-based, asset-light deployment model.

Based on my breakeven projections, D-Wave Quantum is not expected to reach operational profitability until FY2031. This outlook is contingent on the company’s capability to sustain ARR driven by an increased uptake in QCaaS subscriptions. Despite the YoY improvements in EBIT, the current cash holding seems insufficient with the potential for the existing ~320m cash to be drawn down by FY2026. At this key year, the business has two ways to sustain itself. Either it significantly increases ARR generation or continues to conduct capital raises until it reaches profitability. The purpose of this breakeven analysis is to underscore the importance of D-Wave’s management discretion on the commercialisation runway of the product and prudent cash management to reach profitability in the next 8-10 year horizon.

While my view remains bearish on assuming a speculative investment in D-Wave, it is also important to recognise the company’s progress. This is exemplified by the excerpt below from the Q4 2024 investor presentation where D-Wave has made incremental improvements in several financial metrics that suggest early signs of momentum and traction.

From above, D-Wave’s quarterly and annual bookings have increased at a strong pace (~500% quarterly, +100% annual) indicating robust commercial interest in D-Wave’s offerings. However, it is important to distinguish between bookings, which are often one-time discovery or professional service agreements and ARR. The ARR component remains limited at this point in the stage, but the strong increase in bookings will likely flow down to recurring revenue.

One of the more encouraging elements of the FY2024 financial update, from my perspective, is the decline in secured debt, which appears to be on full track for repayment. This, alongside the large cash position discussed previously, provides a strong liquidity profile, which shall increase the breakeven runway, assuming continued progress on recurring revenue generation. Strengthening the balance sheet remains a prudential move due to the lingering anticipation of macroeconomic headwinds likely to affect both the U.S. and global markets due to trade policy uncertainty.

Nevertheless, the pressure to demonstrate a clear path to profitability remains acute. Frequent capital raises, while necessary for early-stage growth companies, can often negatively impact investor sentiment and raise questions about the business's ability to develop self-sustaining revenue streams. Hence, if the capital raises are not paired with reliable commercial traction, repeated equity dilution will challenge long-term shareholder confidence increasing stock volatility and depressing share price.

2. D-Wave’s Quantum Annealing technology remains an implementation with limited use cases

D-Wave is a pioneering company within the quantum computing industry, particularly known for its quantum annealing technology, distinct from traditional quantum computing methodologies. Major industry incumbents such as mega-cap technology like Google, Microsoft, and IBM and larger pure-play players like IONQ and Rigetti focus on gate-based quantum computing methodologies. Both quantum annealing and gate-based quantum computing leverage the principles of quantum mechanics, notably the ability of quantum bits (Qubits) to exist in superposition, qubits remain superimposed such that both bits 0 and 1 are available at the same time e.g., 11, 00, 10,01. This is different from classical bits, which can represent either 0 or 1 at a given time.

Rather than dwelling on the complex dynamics of the technology, the prudent consideration by investors is to understand the practical use cases and unique functions of each type of quantum computer. Quantum annealing is particularly well suited for solving optimization problems, where the goal is to determine the best solution from a set of solutions. Optimization remains a significant challenge across industries, though the application of which tends to be in specialized niches rather than broad approaches. Some real-world use cases involve:

  • Portfolio optimization: Determining the most efficient asset allocation through methods like mean-variance optimization, ultimately utilised to balance expected returns and risk.
  • Supply Chain & Logistic Optimization: Enhancing routing strategies and inventory management across distribution networks could potentially lead to substantial cost savings.
  • Pharmaceutical research: Accelerating drug discovery through identifying optimal molecular structures, thereby reducing R&D timelines.

While these applications underscore the value of quantum annealing, their scope remains quite narrow when compared with gate-based methodologies. Gate-based methodologies can address a wide range of computational challenges beyond optimization, such as the advancement of machine learning and artificial intelligence techniques through the rapid execution of linear algebra computations.

Given the wide array of applications, gate-based quantum computing can tackle, it is considered to be the default technology when companies seek to utilise quantum computation. So unless Quantum annealing greatly trumps gate-based methodology, D-Wave will be confined to a limited portion of quantum computing market share.

A significant hurdle for D-Wave is its limited commercial adoption of its technology, while the company has attracted interest from several large enterprises. Its financial disclosures state that these remain in the pilot or discovery stages, while few are committing to subscription service. This sales-pipeline model implies that most discovery engagements are a vulnerable element of D-Wave’s revenues, particularly in times when corporates choose to cut costs, as pilot projects are often amongst the first expenditures to be reduced.

There should be given recognition for D-Wave’s capability to secure numerous partnerships, collaborations, and discovery engagements, but there remains a low conversion rate into ARR. In contrast, large-cap players like IBM, Microsoft, and Amazon benefit from extensive, diversified revenue bases and the ability to integrate quantum computing into their existing cloud infrastructure. This not only allows them to develop innovative solutions for their customers through a range of products but also utilises revenue from other business segments to sustain longer and deeper R&D investment cycles.

It seems plausible that gate-based quantum computing shall command an increasing share of the market, as quantum annealing remains a specialised tool for optimization problems in this scenario D-Wave will struggle to develop sustainable profitability and growth into the long term amidst other larger players.

3. D-Wave’s lackluster competitive positioning is likely to not improve

A significant challenge for D-Wave’s competitive positioning lies in the entrenched dominance of major technology companies like IBM, Google, etc. These firms benefit from immense R&D budgets with the capability to easily sustain their R&D pipelines into the foreseeable future, positioning them to lead quantum computing commercialisation. If Gate-based quantum computing can reach wider commercial viability within 5-10 years, this will pose a significant threat to D-Wave’s current value proposition. D-Wave risks being sidelined, as enterprise adoption shifts to flexible cloud adoption rather than deployed physical systems, many Mega Cap technology names already possess cloud infrastructure, which allows their solutions to be easily integrated.

Below is a simplified peer comparable valuation on D-Wave Quantum, with its peer set both its direct peers alongside large-cap peers. We see that it sets around the middle of its peer group competing with positioning against Rigetti Computing mainly. The peer valuation also demonstrates the speculative nature of the company in comparison to large-cap peers (which is my preferred way to play the quantum computing trend).

Above is a relatively simple peer comps table, which evaluates D-Wave Quantum to its direct peers and then large-cap peers. The direct pure-play quantum computing companies remain at extreme valuations often in the triple digits for EV/Revenue and P/S ratios. This means that investors are pricing in significant future growth for the companies, albeit the negative net income positions mean that the companies remain speculative, with an excessive reliance on equity financing to sustain operations. From the direct peers, IonQ seems to have the strongest fundamental narrative, with strong growth, the lowest negative net income, and more stable multiples in comparison. When we look at the large-cap peers, we see that risk-adjusted positioning favours these companies, mainly Alphabet or Microsoft, as an indirect play to capture quantum computing trends.

4. D-Wave’s path to profitability remains uncertain

In my opinion, the commercial viability of quantum computing remains merely a long-term prospect, with the actual widespread adoption of the technology likely not available until the mid-to-late 2030s. Given the nascent nature of the industry, investments in companies like D-Wave face asymmetrical risk, skewed to the downside. I remain confused about the path of profitability for D-Wave, as it will likely need capital to sustain its operations for at least the next decade, which is likely to increasingly become difficult in a macroeconomic environment that has constrained liquidity. For investors like us, this raises significant concerns about the company's ability to weather the quantum computing industry’s maturation without substantial volatility.

D-Wave’s long-term exit strategy remains quite unclear perhaps the best possible outcome for D-Wave is an acquisition by a larger mega-cap quantum computing player. This might be the most viable way for investors seeking an adequate return on capital since as the quantum computing industry consolidates it is likely to do around these mega-cap players, like Google, Microsoft, Amazon, and IBM, leaving smaller players like D-Wave, Rigetti, and even IONQ at structural disadvantages. While personally for me, acquisition could provide some upside, it remains quite an uncertain outcome and doesn’t warrant taking a highly volatile punt.

Risks to the View

There are always risks to developing an investment thesis in the stock, and some of those factors must be addressed. The main risk here is the successful commercialisation of D-Wave’s Quantum Annealing technology such that wide adoption is generated. This could be one of the cases that play out over the 10-15 years it takes Quantum Computing to mature into commonly used technology, though I assign a low probability to such an outcome. The most favourable outcome for an investor is that tech large cap acquires D-Wave Quantum, which provides instant upside for investors.

There is an opportunity to develop more stronger agreements with companies like Carahsoft, which can help D-Wave gain access to public sector contracts. This ultimately could open a pathway to ARR stability.

Conclusion

I conclude this short snippet with a sell rating. In my view, D-Wave Quantum, along with other smaller quantum computing players like IONQ and Rigetti, represent an asymmetrical risk-reward bet. While D-Wave Quantum has demonstrated some redeeming elements, such as steadfast innovation and 1st mover advantage in quantum annealing, its weak financial stability, limited commercial adoption rates, and intensifying competition from mega-cap technology names significantly undermine its medium-long term investment case.

D-Wave’s current cash runway of ~320m masks deeper issues, a low base of recurring revenue, a hype cycle within quantum computing without actionable use cases outside of randomised probability distributions, and the company’s requirement of a ten-fold increase in sales growth to obtain reach breakeven. If investors decide to invest in D-Wave Quantum they should look out for the following developments:

  • Quarterly progress in revenue growth, specifically booking/engagements to ARR conversion rate.
  • Quantum Annealing & Gate-based development updates.

In my view, the most prudent way to gain exposure to the quantum computing trend would be through mega-cap technology names like IBM, Microsoft, Google, and Amazon, which remain well-capitalised, further advancing gate-based quantum architectures. Unlike pure-play quantum computing firms, these companies are more likely to sustain their R&D ramps well into the future without the same amount of financial strain. This, in my opinion, significantly reduces downside volatility while providing adequate exposure to this emerging technology trend. For investors still invested in speculative quantum investments, the most viable thesis for D-Wave remains the eventual acquisition of its technology and operations by a much larger technology firm. However, such a scenario is merely speculative and not certain.

What Is The Systematic Approach To Investing?

Systematic investing is an active approach to investing that emphasizes data-driven insights, scientific testing of investment ideas, and advanced computer modelling techniques to construct portfolios.

This process includes a comprehensive examination of empirical evidence by seasoned investment experts — testing different combinations of variables and comparing the results to known outcomes. This ability to validate insights means portfolio decisions are firmly evidence-based and not dependent on human conviction alone. Today’s data rich environment — data creation and storage has increased by a factor of 100 over the past 15 years1 — gives power to systematic managers who use large language models (LLMs) and machine learning algorithms to extract signals from a sea of data.

Signal boom

The increase in investment signals used by BlackRock Systematic

There are tens of thousands of data inputs, which can help lead to investment signals. For example, looking across online job postings as COVID-19 lockdowns eased, BlackRock’s systematic team found that demand for dog walkers was a significant data point. This data showed real-time movement toward reopening on a state-by-state level, reflecting a wider availability of vaccines and return to office mandates. This is one example of how we use alternative data sets — in this case, job postings for dog walkers — to uncover timely insights on the economy and companies.

Number of dog-walking postings

To be clear, having expert investors at every step of the process is essential. Systematic investing combines human expertise with technology to bring together the art and the science of investing. We believe the systematic principles of investing at scale with big data, technology and human insight can serve many different investment goals, including seeking alpha — or returns in excess of a broad benchmark — through our exchange traded funds (ETFs).

Outperformance, Income And Other Uses Of Systematic Investing

The consistent outperformance targeted by our systematic ETFs can help boost returns while also reducing overall risk and volatility — desirable outcomes in any market cycle, and especially valuable in today’s environment of heightened volatility.

Here are examples of actively managed iShares ETFs that leverage BlackRock’s systematic platform.

  • The iShares U.S. Thematic Rotation Active ETF (THRO) seeks long-term capital appreciation by dynamically rotating exposure to U.S. market themes. These themes can include both long-term mega forces and shorter-term trends, allowing investors to pursue alpha opportunities as markets evolve.
  • The iShares U.S. Tech Independence Focused ETF (IETC) seeks to capture long-term growth opportunities using alternative data to identify companies across the technology landscape with a greater proportion of capabilities, revenues, and production derived in the U.S. and select global markets.
  • The iShares U.S. Equity Factor Rotation Active ETF (DYNF) seeks to outperform the broad U.S. market by tactically allocating across factors based on real-time macroeconomic and market insights. DYNF dynamically selects stocks based on their exposure to specific factors — such as quality, value, momentum, growth, or low volatility — that the portfolio managers believe will do best based on forward-looking insights.
  • The iShares Advantage Large Cap Income ETF (BALI) uses the systematic team’s proprietary dividend rotation model to invest in a basket of large-cap stocks. The strategy seeks to enhance income by writing monthly call options on the S&P 500 Index while maintaining upside market participation.

Conclusion

Systematic investing has a rich history of evolution. At its core, it combines rigorous thinking and economic intuition with advanced forecasting techniques - including statistical analysis, economic modeling, and now, machine learning and artificial intelligence. These methods are applied to an ever-expanding universe of data sources. In today’s digital age that data goes beyond traditional financial information to capture sources like news stories, web traffic, social media sentiment, and consumer geolocation patterns.

We see a world of opportunity in systematic investing. Combining human insight with the power of AI and big data brings new levels of precision and efficiency to the investment process - helping uncover actionable insights and drive better portfolio outcomes.
