data.title: What's Driving Bonds And Where Might They Be Headed?
data.description:
By Matthew Brill, Head of North America Investment Grade Credit and Craig Altholz, Client Portfolio Manager

With geopolitical uncertainties influencing credit markets, we discuss the various market drivers and where we think bonds may be headed in the coming months.

Craig: There’s been a lot of speculation about a buyer’s strike on US Treasuries, bonds, and equities. What’s your take?

Matt: We're not seeing a buyer strike, or even a buyer's pause. We're just seeing a little bit more competition. Yields are higher in a lot of different places, particularly in Japan, so they’re competing with US assets. That’s opposed to the last several years where they were the only game in town because of negative yielding debt around the globe. Also, there are some tax policies in the Trump administration’s “Big Beautiful Bill” that have the potential to deter foreigner investors. Also, there was an initial shock from tariffs and concern around volatility. There’s still uncertainty, but the market got a better feel for where Trump may be headed, and overall, there's still a huge demand for US Treasurys and fixed income.2

Craig: What's your thinking about the US Federal Reserve (Fed) cutting rates?

Matt: The market is still pricing in two cuts, but I think the feeling has increasingly been that we might not see any cuts this year. We’ve seen better-than-expected labor market statistics and investors are still concerned about inflation given the tariff uncertainty. If the Fed doesn’t cut rates this year, it generally will mean that the US economy has been better than people have been fearing. That's starting to look like it might be a possibility. On the other hand, if we were to go into a recession near term, the Fed could quickly cut three or four times to kick-start the economy. Overall, our opinion is that the economy is actually better than people had feared. We’ve now had three recessions predicted in just the last three years, and zero of them have played out.

Craig: What are your thoughts about positioning on the yield curve?

Matt: We had a curve steepening, which means we were long on the front end of the curve while being short on the long end of the curve.3 That helped performance. I think it makes sense to still be slightly overweight in the front end based on where we are now. As I said, we do think the economy is better than most people fear. However, if we are wrong and there is a recession, we have an overweight in credit assets. Credit spreads would widen in that scenario, but the curve positioning could somewhat offset that move if the Fed reduced rates. At the same time, we are cautious at the long end because the budget deficit is not going to get any better anytime soon.

Craig: What are your thoughts about bond returns for the rest of the year?

Matt: Annualized returns for fixed income since Q4, 2024 have been around 5.5-6%.4 But it hasn't felt good, in our view, because there are concerns around the budget deficit, inflation, and whether the Fed will cut or not. It's been volatile and there’s been a lot of uncertainty.

But you still have attractive yields, and that’s what matters most. The yield on the Bloomberg US Corporate Bond Index coming into the year was 5.3%. I think the yield on the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index was about 4.9%. Those are the highest they've been in over 15 years.5 So a good way to think about bonds in our view is that you've gotten a better-than-expected return this year and your yield is still good, so you haven't missed the trade.

Craig: Is the market being too optimistic from a credit spread perspective? How do you think about the next leg of the corporate bond universe?

Matt: It's certainly an unloved bond market right now from a credit standpoint, in our view. I think a lot of investors wish there was more of a credit spread because there’s still uncertainty. However, yields are what's driving inflows, and it’s driving buyers to say “I can buy a high quality single A credit for 10 years at 5.5%. Why would I not want to do that?” We wish spreads were wider, even at their worst, we’re looking at them and saying, “well, they're not pricing in a recession. What happens if it occurs?” Overall, we believe the worst is behind us from a volatility standpoint, which generally means spreads should have a room to run from here.

Footnotes

  1. Source: Bloomberg L.P.. Year-end Yield to Worst on the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index was 4.91%. In a flat environment you would expect the index to produce 2.04% year to date returns as of May 31 on a steady run rate; in actuality the index returned 2.45% as of May 31.
  2. Source: Bloomberg “Investors Snap Up Five-Year Treasuries in Show of Solid Demand” published May 25, 2025.
  3. A curve steepener is a trading strategy where an investor aims to profit from an increase in the yield difference between short and long-term bonds. If the yield curve steepens, it means the difference between long-term and short-term yields is increasing. This can happen because long-term yields rise more than short-term yields, or short-term yields fall more than long-term yields. "Long front end" means buying short-term bonds. If interest rates fall, bond prices rise, so you profit if short-term rates fall. "Short back end" means selling (or shorting) long-term bonds. If interest rates rise, bond prices fall, so you profit if long-term rates rise less than short-term rates.
  4. Source: Bloomberg L.P., Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index and Bloomberg US Corporate Total Return Index returns as of March 31st, 2025.
  5. Source: Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index Yield to Worst return was 4.91 as of 12/31/2024, Bloomberg US Corporate Bond Index Yield to Worst was 5.33% as of 12/31/2024.

Important information

Image: Roberto Moiola / Sysaworld / Getty

Most references are US-centric and may not apply to Canada. All data is in USD unless otherwise stated.

All investing involves risk, including the risk of loss.

Past performance does not guarantee future results.

Investments cannot be made directly in an index.

This does not constitute a recommendation of any investment strategy or product for a particular investor. Investors should consult a financial professional before making any investment decisions.

Fixed income investments are subject to credit risk of the issuer and the effects of changing interest rates. Interest rate risk refers to the risk that bond prices generally fall as interest rates rise and vice versa. An issuer may be unable to meet interest and/or principal payments, thereby causing its instruments to decrease in value and lowering the issuer’s credit rating.

The Bloomberg US Corporate Bond Index measures the investment grade, fixed-rate, taxable corporate bond market. It includes USD denominated securities publicly issued by US and non-US industrial, utility and financial issuers.

The Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index is an unmanaged index considered representative of the US investment-grade, fixed-rate bond market.

The Bloomberg FEDL01 Index represents the average interest rate at which commercial banks borrow and lend excess reserves to each other overnight. It's a key indicator of the cost of borrowing for banks and is heavily influenced by the Federal Reserve's target rate. The Federal Reserve uses this rate as a primary tool to implement monetary policy and manage the overall economy. The Bloomberg USGG2YR Index represents the yield received for investing in a US government issued treasury security with a maturity of 2 years. It is a key indicator of the shorter end of the yield curve and is considered important for understanding the overall US economy. The Bloomberg USGG30YR Index represents the U.S. Generic 30-year Treasury Yield. This index provides a historical rate for the 30-year U.S. Treasury, derived from market data, as opposed to the mathematically calculated "par-yield" provided by the U.S. Department of the Treasury. Inflation is the rate at which the general price level for goods and services is increasing.

Credit spread is the difference in yield between bonds of similar maturity but with different credit quality.

The yield curve plots interest rates, at a set point in time, of bonds having equal credit quality but differing maturity dates to project future interest rate changes and economic activity.

The opinions referenced above are those of the author as of June 6, 2025 and are based on market conditions as of May 31, 2025. These opinions may differ from those of other Invesco investment professionals and are subject to change without notice.

These comments should not be construed as recommendations, but as an illustration of broader themes. Forward-looking statements are not guarantees of future results. They involve risks, uncertainties and assumptions; there can be no assurance that actual results will not differ materially from expectations.

data.title: 2 Hidden Stocks Riding One Of The Biggest Booms Of Our Lifetime
data.description:
Introduction

Earlier this month, I wrote an article providing an update on my "Big Picture" research framework, which is the foundation of almost every financial decision I make, both on and off the stock market.

As we can see below, it's based on three major pillars:

  • Company-specific factors (this used to be "supply chains")
  • Economic Fundamentals (Fmr. "macro")
  • External Risks & Opportunities (Fmr. "politics")

Usually, I use this framework to discuss dividend stocks. In this article, I'll discuss two non-dividend-paying stocks. While this may potentially upset some of my income-focused followers, I can say I am NOT pivoting away from my core strategy, which is to invest every penny of spare cash in dividend (growth) stocks.

The goal of this article is to shed more light on one of the biggest economic trends at the moment, which will impact every single investor, regardless of age, strategy, and wealth status.

On top of that, I'm using it to expand my coverage, as I want to provide even more food for thought for an even bigger audience.

So, while we'll get back to income stocks tomorrow, this is the kind of article/content/info that I consider to be critical for everyone, as it allows us to assess our existing positions and make educated judgments about new investments and potential investment opportunities.

So, without further ado, let's jump right in!

Nuclear, It's Happening

I was close to turning 16 when the Fukushima nuclear accident happened in March 2011. Back then, I just started trading the markets (with very little knowledge and a huge ego), and curiously followed the reaction of market participants and politicians.

It was a tricky time, as Europe was just in the midst of a sovereign debt crisis, which occurred just two years after the Great Financial Crisis. Needless to say, most people were nervous. The first major nuclear accident since Chernobyl made this even worse, as it caused major economies to rethink their energy policies. This included Germany, which decided to say goodbye to the cleanest and most dense form of energy known to mankind.

Coupled with the nation's push into renewables, this turned out to be one of the biggest mistakes in its modern history, as it made Europe's biggest industrial nation prone to disruption, as the massive surge in energy inflation after the pandemic shows. After more than a decade of basically zero energy inflation, the absence of reliable energy caused its economic foundation to crumble, triggering a wave of bankruptcies and core economic trouble.

Now, nuclear is making a comeback, as even Germany has realized it may have made a mistake in light of the global boom in power demand.

With that said, nuclear energy has been a topic I have discussed for years. At the risk of sounding cocky, I think we have been on this trend before it became mainstream.

However, it is becoming such a big trend that it requires even more attention.

After all, in the current surge for power to support global electrification and next-gen technologies like advanced artificial intelligence, we need reliable and affordable energy. Nuclear energy isn't just dense, but it is also reliable, as nuclear power plants produce maximum power more than 92% of the time (the so-called capacity factor), according to EIA data.

According to Goldman Sachs' latest report, nuclear power is expected to see a rise from 378 gigawatts to 575 gigawatts by 2040, with an increasing number of countries pledging to triple nuclear power capacity by 2050.

Although the nuclear buildout continued after 2011, the share of nuclear power dropped to just 9% of the global power supply. That's down from the all-time high peak of 17% in the 1980s.

Increasing demand and regulatory support, such as President Trump's executive order aimed at reducing regulatory and cost barriers for new construction, create favorable conditions for new development.

While nuclear waste is still an issue, benefits like unmatched energy density, reliability, and no carbon emissions make the power superior and a great addition to natural gas reactors, which I am also extremely bullish on.

It also helps that lower regulatory hurdles and massive investment flows support technological advancements, creating new tailwinds like the future adoption of small modular reactors ("SMR").

New technologies are also set to boost nuclear power generation capacity. As investment in nuclear energy has started to pick up globally, countries are allocating money not only to large nuclear reactors but also to newer variants such as small modular reactors (SMRs). SMRs are a fraction of the size of a larger nuclear power plant, and they’re comprised of standardized components that can be mass-produced in traditional facilities and shipped to the site, unlike traditional reactors which are built on-site with larger components. SMRs typically have a lower power generation capacity (between 20-300 megawatts, compared to around 1GW for traditional nuclear reactors). - Goldman Sachs (emphasis added)

Although it will likely take until the late 2030s for SMRs to become mainstream, which is why natural gas is so critical, we are truly looking at one of the most favorable periods for nuclear energy adoption and innovation.

In fact, demand will be so high that the uranium supply is unlikely to keep up. That's why alternatives will remain critical and won't get replaced by nuclear energy for many more decades.

On the other hand, uranium production is set to increase from its current 80,000 tons to nearly 95,000 tons in 2030, before eventually declining to around 60,000 tons by 2045. In aggregate, the team is forecasting a uranium supply deficit of roughly 17,500 tons by 2030. “We anticipate this deficit to grow to roughly 100,000 tons by 2045 as new reactors come online,” write Lee and Davenport. - Goldman Sachs (emphasis added)

That's why I have been bullish on Cameco Corp. (CCJ) for many years, Canada's largest uranium miner and one of the biggest producers of nuclear fuel for Western companies. This has made it a beneficiary of rising nuclear power demand and increasing geopolitical risks.

Since my first article on the stock in November 2022, the company's stock price has nearly tripled, making it one of my best alternative energy tips.

However, while I remain bullish on this company, the two picks I am primarily discussing in this article are lesser-known companies I wanted to bring to everyone's attention.

Talen Energy Corp. (TLN) - Top-Tier Energy Infrastructure

Talen is a leading independent power producer and infrastructure company that owns and operates roughly 10.7 gigawatts of power infrastructure in the United States, including 2.2 gigawatts of nuclear power and a major dispatchable generation fleet.

It produces and sells electricity, capacity, and related services into wholesale markets, with most of its assets in the Mid-Atlantic region and Montana.

Related to the nuclear energy boom and data center demand, Talen is also powering what it calls the "digital infrastructure revolution." It is in a great position to capture this significant growth opportunity, as data centers serving artificial intelligence rely on dependable and very reliable energy.

In the first quarter, the company saw a 3.5% increase in weather-adjusted power deliveries in the PJM operating region, driving 1.6 TWh of additional generation from its fossil fleet. Essentially, this supports the company's view that rising energy demand will increase the dispatch of its flexible fossil assets. It also supports my thesis that every molecule of energy is needed.

Because of these qualities, the company is working closely with hyperscalers, which are some of the market's biggest companies. This includes a deal with Amazon (AMZN), to serve data center load, putting Talen in a position as a differentiated independent power producer.

Essentially, the deal shifts from a behind-the-meter to a front-of-the-meter (grid-connected) arrangement, with Talen as the retail provider for Amazon's Susquehanna data center and other Pennsylvania sites.

To add some more details, during this month's investor call, the company noted the agreement eliminates the need for FERC approval and addresses regulatory challenges faced after an ISA amendment rejection in 2024.

The transition to grid-connected delivery requires a minor electrical setup, expected during a 2026 refueling outage, with no slowdown in campus construction. Also, the contract expands the power purchase agreement ("PPA") to nearly 2 gigawatts (up from 960 MW) and unlocks the company's second nuclear unit that it previously held in reserve.

As one can imagine, this is very bullish for the company's financials, as it is expecting more than 50% growth in after-tax free cash flow per share by 2032, adding more than $8 to its 2026 guidance.

At full capacity, the deal is expected to generate $1.4 billion per year, with a total notional value of $18 billion over the full 17 years of the contracts. This shows just how HUGE this data center buildout is for companies further down the supply chain.

Between 2024 and 2032, the company aims for 20% annual growth in per-share post-tax free cash flow, one of the highest numbers across all companies on my radar. This, too, is a huge deal.

It will also allow the company to generate 70% of its income from long-term contracts and stable sources by 2032. In 2026, that number is expected to be just 40%. In other words, cash flow volatility will rapidly decline going forward.

So, what does this mean for its valuation?

Talen isn't super cheap.

However, it's far from overvalued, as we are dealing with a company capable of long-term double-digit annual free cash flow growth trading at less than 23x earnings. As a result, I expect the stock price to rise by 10-20% per year, supported by what I forecast to be a very rapid buildout of nuclear power capabilities and customers who look for direct solutions that Talen offers.

As such, Talen remains a Buy.

The same goes for the next pick.

Centrus Energy Corp. (LEU) - A Small Nuclear Gem You Probably Didn't Know

Centrus Energy is one of the companies I wish I had found out about earlier, as it's a fascinating player in the nuclear industry.

Headquartered in Bethesda, Maryland, the company is a leader in the supply of nuclear fuel and services, with a specialization in uranium enrichment for the nuclear power industry.

Even better, as the only U.S.-owned company currently enriching uranium with domestically developed technology, the company is a "mission-critical" player that supports areas like clean energy, energy security, and a wide range of other national security objectives.

Moreover, as we can see in the overview above, it services its customers through two major segments:

  • Low-Enriched Uranium ("LEU") provides nuclear fuel components like separative work units and enriched uranium products to utilities. It's also where the company got its ticker, which is why there is sometimes some confusion (I'll make sure to avoid it).

  • Technical Solutions offers engineering, manufacturing, and operational services to public and private sector clients. According to the company, since 1998, it has supplied more than 1,850 reactor years of fuel, which is the equivalent of more than 7 billion tons of coal.

Now, the company is working on pioneering the production of HALEU, which stands for High-Assay, Low-Enriched Uranium, which is a critical fuel for next-generation advanced reactors. The company's facility in Ohio is the first one in the U.S. using technology from the U.S. to boost production in almost 70 years.

In other words, on top of benefiting from the boom in nuclear energy demand, the company is also critical in America's effort to become less dependent on nations like Russia, which dominates the uranium supply through proxies like Kazakhstan.

The company also agrees with my thesis, citing various sources that see:

  • 40% growth in nuclear energy over the next three decades without new climate policies.
  • The need to triple nuclear power generation to achieve emission targets by 2050. While I doubt these targets will matter a lot when push comes to shove, it shows none of these targets can be achieved without nuclear power.
  • 90% of all nuclear reactors of the Advanced Reactor Demonstration Program operate on HALEU, the commodity Centrus is pioneering.

Total HALEU demand is expected to rise from 26 metric tons in 2025 to more than 610 metric tons by 2035. As Centrus is the only company with an NRC license for this material, it could benefit from a $6.2 billion HALEU market by 2035 like no other player.

It also helps that Centrus has a strong relationship with federal organizations, as federal investments in HALEU and LEU (the material) are expected to be a $3.4 billion catalyst, with national security demand outpacing global utility demand, which is something a lot of people seem to overlook.

During its 1Q25 earnings call, the company explained it has secured three DOE contracts for HALEU production, HALEU deconversion, and LEU production, which is supported by more than $3.4 billion in congressional appropriations, including $700 million from the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022.

Essentially, these contracts position the company to benefit from significant federal investment aimed at restoring domestic nuclear fuel production.

It currently has a backlog of $3.8 billion.

Moreover:

  • It has a balance sheet with more than $650 million in unrestricted cash.
  • Its net income has grown by 29% per year since 2017.

The problem is putting a valuation on LEU. It's the kind of company analysts usually struggle to value, as many contracts are long-term and can be influenced by new policy changes. Every new contract could meaningfully boost the long-term growth trajectory.

Currently trading at a blended P/E ratio of 41x earnings, LEU isn't cheap. However, I do not consider it expensive, as it has a wide-moat business model, a critical position in America's national security, and a front seat in the nuclear boom.

While I cannot give a clear price target, I apply a Buy rating due to my bullish view on the industry, its business model, and the company's 41x multiple.

Takeaway

Nuclear energy is back, and it's bigger than ever.

After years of being sidelined by mostly Western nations since Fukushima, the world is waking up to the fact that we need reliable, dense, and clean power to fuel AI, electrification, and general economic growth.

One could say there is no way around nuclear energy.

In this article, I highlighted two non-dividend stocks, which are Talen Energy and Centrus Energy, which are poised to capitalize on this megatrend.

Talen's deal with Amazon underscores the critical role of nuclear in powering data centers, while Centrus is a critical player in America's push for energy independence through HALEU production.

Moreover, while both offer explosive growth potential, they come with volatility.

All things considered, whether you invest in these names or not, understanding the nuclear renaissance is key to navigating the next decade, which is why I will put a lot more emphasis on Big Picture developments.
